metadata.title: C/C++ Mastery Guide for Senior Engineers
metadata.description: Comprehensive C/C++ concepts covering pointers, memory management, bit manipulation, OOP, templates, STL, concurrency, and modern C++ features. Designed for senior engineers preparing for roles at Nvidia, Google, Qualcomm, Ericsson, and Arista Networks.
metadata.targetAudience: Senior Software Engineers (5+ years) refreshing C/C++ for big tech interviews

chapters.title: Pointers Fundamentals
chapters.description: Master raw pointers, pointer arithmetic, function pointers, and common pointer pitfalls in C/C++.

topics.title: Raw Pointers & Pointer Declaration
topics.explanation:
A pointer stores the memory address of another variable. The type of a pointer determines how the memory at that address is interpreted. Pointers are fundamental to C/C++ and are used for dynamic memory, data structures, and hardware interaction.

Declaration: `int *p;` declares a pointer to int. The `*` binds to the variable name, not the type, so `int *a, b;` declares a pointer `a` and an int `b`.
keyPoints: A pointer holds a memory address, not a value
The size of a pointer depends on the architecture (4 bytes on 32-bit, 8 bytes on 64-bit)
Dereferencing (*p) accesses the value at the address stored in p
The address-of operator (&) gives the address of a variable
Uninitialized pointers contain garbage addresses — always initialize to nullptr

codeExamples.title: Basic Pointer Operations
codeExamples.explanation: Demonstrates declaration, address-of (&), dereferencing (*), and modifying a value through a pointer.

codeExamples.title: Pointer Size Across Types
codeExamples.explanation: All pointer types have the same size on a given platform — it's the address size, not the pointed-to type size.

commonMistakes: Declaring int* a, b; thinking both are pointers (only a is a pointer)
Using uninitialized pointers (wild pointers) leading to undefined behavior
Confusing * in declaration (pointer type) vs * in expression (dereference)
interviewTips: Know the difference between pointer declaration and dereferencing
Be ready to explain why all pointer types have the same sizeof
Understand that pointers are just integers holding addresses

topics.title: Pointer Arithmetic
topics.explanation:
Pointer arithmetic allows navigating memory by adding or subtracting integers from pointers. The key insight: when you add 1 to a pointer, it advances by sizeof(pointed_type) bytes, not 1 byte. This is why the type of a pointer matters.

Subtracting two pointers of the same type gives the number of elements between them (ptrdiff_t).
keyPoints: p + n advances the pointer by n * sizeof(*p) bytes
Pointer subtraction (p2 - p1) gives the number of elements between two pointers
Pointer arithmetic is only defined within arrays or one-past-the-end
You can compare pointers with <, >, ==, != within the same array
Incrementing a char* moves by 1 byte; incrementing an int* moves by 4 bytes (typically)

codeExamples.title: Pointer Arithmetic with Arrays
codeExamples.explanation: Shows how pointer arithmetic navigates arrays and that pointer difference gives element count, not byte count.

commonMistakes: Assuming p+1 adds 1 byte (it adds sizeof(*p) bytes)
Performing arithmetic on void* (not allowed in C++, allowed as GCC extension in C)
Pointer arithmetic beyond array bounds is undefined behavior
interviewTips: Classic interview question: What does arr[i] actually mean? Answer: *(arr + i)
Know that arr[i] == i[arr] because both expand to *(arr + i)
Understand ptrdiff_t as the type for pointer differences

topics.title: Pointer to Pointer & Void Pointers
topics.explanation:
A pointer to pointer (double pointer) stores the address of another pointer. This is used for modifying pointer values inside functions, 2D dynamic arrays, and argv in main().

A void pointer (void*) is a generic pointer that can hold the address of any type but cannot be dereferenced directly — you must cast it first. It's the basis of C's generic programming (malloc returns void*).
keyPoints: int **pp declares a pointer to a pointer to int
Used in functions that need to modify a pointer argument
void* can hold any pointer type but cannot be dereferenced or used in arithmetic
C allows implicit void* conversion; C++ requires explicit cast
char **argv in main is a pointer to an array of char pointers

codeExamples.title: Pointer to Pointer — Modifying a Pointer in a Function
codeExamples.explanation: Double pointer allows a function to allocate memory and update the caller's pointer. Without **, the function would only modify a local copy.

codeExamples.title: Void Pointer — Generic Swap
codeExamples.explanation: void* enables type-agnostic functions in C-style code. In C++, templates are preferred for this.

commonMistakes: Dereferencing void* without casting — compilation error
Confusing int **pp with int (*pp)[N] (pointer to array)
Forgetting that C++ requires explicit cast from void* unlike C
interviewTips: Explain when you'd use void* vs templates (C compatibility, runtime type erasure)
Know why main(int argc, char **argv) uses double pointer
Be able to trace through double pointer dereferencing step by step

topics.title: Const Pointers & Function Pointers
topics.explanation:
The const keyword with pointers has two independent dimensions:
- `const int *p` (or `int const *p`): pointer to const — can't modify the value through p
- `int *const p`: const pointer — can't change what p points to
- `const int *const p`: both — can't modify value or change pointer

Function pointers store addresses of functions, enabling callbacks, strategy pattern, and jump tables. Read declarations right-to-left.
keyPoints: Read right-to-left: 'int *const p' = p is a const pointer to int
const int* and int const* are identical
Function pointer syntax: returnType (*name)(paramTypes)
typedef/using simplifies function pointer declarations
std::function is the modern C++ alternative to function pointers

codeExamples.title: Const Correctness with Pointers
codeExamples.explanation: The three levels of const with pointers. Read the declaration right-to-left to understand what's const.

codeExamples.title: Function Pointers and Callbacks
codeExamples.explanation: Function pointers enable callbacks and strategy pattern. std::function is more flexible (accepts lambdas with captures).

commonMistakes: Confusing const int* with int* const
Forgetting that function name decays to function pointer automatically
Trying to use a capturing lambda where a function pointer is expected
interviewTips: Practice reading complex pointer declarations right-to-left
Know when to use function pointers vs std::function vs templates
Understand the const_cast use case: removing const for legacy C APIs

topics.title: Dangling, Wild, and Null Pointers
topics.explanation:
Three dangerous pointer states:

1. **Dangling pointer**: Points to memory that has been freed/deallocated or a local variable that went out of scope. Dereferencing is undefined behavior.

2. **Wild pointer**: An uninitialized pointer containing a garbage address. Dereferencing is undefined behavior.

3. **Null pointer**: Explicitly set to nullptr (C++) or NULL (C). Dereferencing crashes (segfault) but is at least deterministic. Always prefer nullptr over NULL in C++.

nullptr has type std::nullptr_t and avoids ambiguity with integer 0 that NULL has.
keyPoints: Always initialize pointers (to nullptr if no valid address yet)
Set pointers to nullptr after delete to avoid dangling
Returning address of local variable creates a dangling pointer
Use-after-free is a critical security vulnerability (CWE-416)
nullptr is type-safe; NULL is just 0 and can cause overload ambiguity

codeExamples.title: Dangling Pointer Scenarios
codeExamples.explanation: Shows three common pointer bugs: use-after-free, returning local address, and wild pointers. Always initialize and nullify after delete.

commonMistakes: Not setting pointer to nullptr after delete
Returning address of stack variable from a function
Using NULL instead of nullptr in C++ (ambiguity with int overloads)
Double-free: deleting the same pointer twice
interviewTips: Explain the difference between dangling, wild, and null pointers
Know that smart pointers (unique_ptr, shared_ptr) solve most of these problems
Understand why use-after-free is a security vulnerability

quizQuestions.question: What is the output of this code?
quizQuestions.explanation: *p dereferences to x (value 5), adds 10, and stores 15 back through the pointer. x is now 15.

quizQuestions.question: What does `int *a, b;` declare?
options: A pointer to int and an int
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: A pointer to int and an int
quizQuestions.explanation: The * binds to the variable name, not the type. Only 'a' is a pointer; 'b' is a regular int. To declare two pointers: int *a, *b;

quizQuestions.question: What is the output?
True or False: sizeof(int*) is always equal to sizeof(double*) on any given platform.
quizQuestions.explanation: All pointer types have the same size on a given platform since they all store memory addresses of the same width.

quizQuestions.question: Which declaration means 'p is a constant pointer to a non-constant int'?
quizQuestions.explanation: Read right-to-left: 'p is const, pointer to int'. The pointer itself can't change, but the int it points to can be modified.

quizQuestions.question: What is the output?
quizQuestions.explanation: *(p+1) dereferences to 'e'. p+1 is a char* pointing to 'ello', which prints as the C-string 'ello'. Combined output: 'eello'.

chapters.title: Smart Pointers
chapters.description: Modern C++ memory management with unique_ptr, shared_ptr, and weak_ptr — RAII-based automatic resource management.

topics.title: std::unique_ptr — Exclusive Ownership
topics.explanation:
unique_ptr represents exclusive ownership of a dynamically allocated object. Only one unique_ptr can own a resource at a time. When the unique_ptr goes out of scope, it automatically deletes the managed object.

Key: unique_ptr cannot be copied, only moved. This enforces single ownership at compile time.
keyPoints: Exclusive ownership — only one unique_ptr owns the resource
Cannot be copied (copy constructor/assignment deleted)
Can be moved with std::move() to transfer ownership
Use std::make_unique<T>(args) for exception-safe creation (C++14)
Zero overhead compared to raw pointer — no reference counting
Supports custom deleters and array types unique_ptr<T[]>

codeExamples.title: unique_ptr Basics
codeExamples.explanation: unique_ptr automatically frees memory when it goes out of scope. Move semantics transfer ownership cleanly.

commonMistakes: Trying to copy a unique_ptr (use std::move instead)
Using raw new instead of make_unique (exception safety issue)
Accessing a unique_ptr after moving it (it's nullptr)
interviewTips: Explain why unique_ptr has zero overhead vs raw pointers
Know when to use unique_ptr vs shared_ptr
unique_ptr is the default smart pointer — use it unless sharing is needed

topics.title: std::shared_ptr — Shared Ownership
topics.explanation:
shared_ptr uses reference counting to allow multiple pointers to share ownership of an object. The object is deleted when the last shared_ptr owning it is destroyed.

Internally maintains two counts: strong count (shared_ptrs) and weak count (weak_ptrs). The control block stores these counts and the deleter.
keyPoints: Multiple shared_ptrs can own the same object
Uses reference counting — object deleted when count reaches 0
Control block overhead: ~16-32 bytes per managed object
make_shared is more efficient (single allocation for object + control block)
Thread-safe reference counting (atomic increment/decrement)
NOT thread-safe for the pointed-to object itself

codeExamples.title: shared_ptr Reference Counting
codeExamples.explanation: Reference count tracks how many shared_ptrs own the object. Object is deleted when count reaches zero.

commonMistakes: Creating two shared_ptrs from the same raw pointer (double delete!)
Circular references causing memory leaks (use weak_ptr to break cycles)
Using shared_ptr when unique_ptr suffices (unnecessary overhead)
interviewTips: Explain the control block and why make_shared is preferred over shared_ptr(new T)
Know the cost: atomic ref count operations, control block allocation
Be ready to discuss thread safety nuances (ref count is atomic, object access is not)

topics.title: std::weak_ptr — Breaking Circular References
topics.explanation:
weak_ptr is a non-owning observer of a shared_ptr-managed object. It doesn't affect the reference count and can detect if the object has been deleted (expired).

Primary use case: breaking circular references in data structures like doubly-linked lists, observer patterns, and caches.
keyPoints: Does not increment the strong reference count
Must be converted to shared_ptr via lock() before use
expired() checks if the managed object still exists
Essential for breaking circular references between shared_ptrs
Used in observer pattern, caches, and parent-child relationships

codeExamples.title: Breaking Circular Reference with weak_ptr
codeExamples.explanation: If prev were a shared_ptr, A and B would form a cycle and never be deleted. weak_ptr breaks the cycle.

commonMistakes: Dereferencing weak_ptr directly (must call lock() first)
Not checking if lock() returns a valid shared_ptr
Using raw pointers instead of weak_ptr for back-references
interviewTips: Classic question: How do you solve circular reference with shared_ptr?
Know the observer pattern implementation with weak_ptr
Understand the difference between expired() and lock()

topics.title: Custom Deleters & make_unique/make_shared
topics.explanation: Smart pointers can use custom deleters for resources that aren't freed with delete (e.g., FILE*, C library handles, OS handles). make_unique and make_shared are factory functions that are exception-safe and (for shared_ptr) more efficient.
keyPoints: Custom deleter: unique_ptr<FILE, decltype(&fclose)> file(fopen(...), fclose)
make_shared combines object and control block in one allocation
make_unique prevents memory leaks in expressions with multiple news
shared_ptr deleter is type-erased (not part of the type)
unique_ptr deleter is part of the type (changes sizeof)

codeExamples.title: Custom Deleter for C Resources
codeExamples.explanation: Custom deleters let smart pointers manage any resource, not just new/delete memory. FILE*, sockets, GPU handles, etc.

commonMistakes: Forgetting that unique_ptr's deleter changes its type
Not using make_shared/make_unique when possible
Creating shared_ptr from raw pointer in multiple places
interviewTips: Explain why make_shared is more efficient (single allocation)
Know the exception safety argument for make_unique
Discuss RAII and how smart pointers generalize it

quizQuestions.question: Which smart pointer allows multiple owners of the same object?
quizQuestions.explanation: shared_ptr uses reference counting to allow shared ownership. unique_ptr has exclusive ownership. weak_ptr is non-owning.

quizQuestions.question: What is the output?
quizQuestions.explanation: After move, sp is null (count 0). sp2 and sp3 share ownership, so sp2.use_count() is 2.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: unique_ptr has zero runtime overhead compared to a raw pointer.
quizQuestions.explanation: unique_ptr with default deleter compiles to the same machine code as raw pointer + delete. No reference counting, no control block.

quizQuestions.question: What problem does weak_ptr solve?
quizQuestions.explanation: When two objects hold shared_ptrs to each other, their reference counts never reach 0. weak_ptr breaks the cycle.

quizQuestions.question: Why is make_shared<T>() preferred over shared_ptr<T>(new T())?
options: It's faster because it combines object and control block into one allocation
It prevents memory leaks in complex expressions
quizQuestions.explanation: make_shared does a single allocation (efficiency) and avoids the leak scenario where new succeeds but shared_ptr construction fails.

chapters.title: Memory Management
chapters.description: Stack vs heap, memory layout, RAII pattern, new/delete vs malloc/free, memory alignment, and leak detection.

topics.title: Stack vs Heap Memory
topics.explanation:
Stack: Fast, automatic (LIFO), limited size (~1-8MB), stores local variables and function call frames. Allocation/deallocation is just pointer movement.

Heap: Slower, manual/dynamic, virtually unlimited, stores dynamically allocated objects. Uses system calls (brk/mmap). Subject to fragmentation.
keyPoints: Stack: O(1) alloc/dealloc, contiguous, cache-friendly, auto lifetime
Heap: O(n) alloc (first-fit/best-fit), fragmentation, manual lifetime
Stack overflow: deep recursion or large local arrays
Heap fragmentation: many small alloc/free cycles leave holes
Modern allocators (jemalloc, tcmalloc) use arenas and thread-local caches

codeExamples.title: Stack vs Heap Allocation
codeExamples.explanation: Stack variables are automatic; heap variables require explicit management. Stack addresses are typically higher than heap on most systems.

commonMistakes: Allocating large arrays on the stack (stack overflow)
Forgetting delete/delete[] for heap allocations
Mixing delete with new[] or delete[] with new
interviewTips: Draw the memory layout: text, data, BSS, heap (grows up), stack (grows down)
Explain why stack is faster (pointer bump vs free-list search)
Know typical stack sizes and how to change them

topics.title: new/delete vs malloc/free
topics.explanation:
C++ new/delete: type-safe, calls constructors/destructors, throws std::bad_alloc on failure.

C malloc/free: returns void*, no constructors, returns NULL on failure.

Never mix them: don't free() memory from new, don't delete memory from malloc.
keyPoints: new calls constructor; malloc only allocates raw memory
delete calls destructor; free only releases memory
new throws std::bad_alloc; malloc returns NULL on failure
new[] pairs with delete[]; new pairs with delete
placement new constructs an object at a specific address
Use nothrow version: new(nothrow) int returns nullptr on failure

codeExamples.title: new/delete vs malloc/free
codeExamples.explanation: new/delete integrate with C++ object model (constructors/destructors). malloc/free are raw memory operations.

commonMistakes: Using delete instead of delete[] for arrays
Mixing malloc with delete or new with free
Forgetting to call destructor explicitly with placement new
interviewTips: Explain the full sequence: new = operator new (allocate) + constructor
Know that operator new can be overloaded globally or per-class
Understand placement new use cases (memory pools, embedded systems)

topics.title: Process Memory Layout
topics.explanation:
A typical process memory layout (low to high address):
1. Text segment: executable code (read-only)
2. Data segment: initialized global/static variables
3. BSS segment: uninitialized global/static variables (zero-initialized)
4. Heap: grows upward, dynamic allocations
5. Memory-mapped region: shared libraries, mmap
6. Stack: grows downward, local variables and call frames
keyPoints: Text segment is read-only and shared between processes
BSS stands for Block Started by Symbol — zero-initialized
Heap grows toward higher addresses
Stack grows toward lower addresses
Stack and heap can collide if both grow too much
Each thread has its own stack but shares the heap

codeExamples.title: Memory Segments Visualization
codeExamples.explanation: Observe the address ranges: code < data/BSS < heap < ... < stack. Static locals go in data segment, not stack.

commonMistakes: Thinking static local variables are on the stack
Not understanding that string literals are in read-only memory
Ignoring that each thread has its own stack
interviewTips: Be able to draw the memory layout diagram from memory
Know where each type of variable lives (global, static, local, dynamic)
Understand virtual memory and page tables at a high level

topics.title: RAII — Resource Acquisition Is Initialization
topics.explanation:
RAII is the most important C++ idiom. Resources (memory, files, locks, sockets) are acquired in the constructor and released in the destructor. This guarantees cleanup even when exceptions occur.

Every smart pointer, std::lock_guard, std::fstream, and std::vector follows RAII.
keyPoints: Acquire resources in constructor, release in destructor
Guarantees cleanup even if exceptions are thrown
Eliminates manual cleanup code and error-prone goto patterns
Smart pointers are RAII wrappers for heap memory
lock_guard/unique_lock are RAII wrappers for mutexes
RAII is sometimes called SBRM (Scope-Bound Resource Management)

codeExamples.title: RAII for File Handling
codeExamples.explanation: RAII ensures the file is always closed, even if an exception occurs between opening and the function returning.

commonMistakes: Not following RAII, using manual cleanup with error-prone if/goto
Forgetting to delete copy operations for non-copyable resources
Calling destructors explicitly (almost never needed)
interviewTips: RAII is THE answer to 'How does C++ manage resources without GC?'
Connect RAII to smart pointers, lock_guard, and the Rule of 5
Explain how RAII interacts with exception handling

topics.title: Memory Alignment & Padding
topics.explanation: CPUs access memory most efficiently at aligned addresses (address divisible by data size). Compilers add padding bytes to structs to maintain alignment. This affects struct size and is crucial for hardware interaction, network protocols, and cache performance.
keyPoints: Alignment requirement: address must be divisible by sizeof(type)
Compiler inserts padding between struct members for alignment
Struct size is rounded up to a multiple of the largest member's alignment
Reorder members largest-to-smallest to minimize padding
#pragma pack or __attribute__((packed)) disables padding (use carefully)
alignas(N) specifies custom alignment (C++11)

codeExamples.title: Struct Padding and Reordering
codeExamples.explanation: Reordering struct members from largest to smallest alignment saves 33% memory here. Critical for cache performance in arrays.

commonMistakes: Assuming struct size equals sum of member sizes
Using packed structs for performance (it actually hurts due to unaligned access)
Not considering alignment in network/file serialization
interviewTips: Be able to calculate struct sizes with padding on the spot
Know alignof() and alignas() operators
Explain why cache line alignment (64 bytes) matters for performance

quizQuestions.question: Where are local variables stored?
quizQuestions.explanation: Local (automatic) variables are stored on the stack and automatically destroyed when the function returns.

quizQuestions.question: What is the output?
quizQuestions.code:
struct S { char a; int b; char c; };
cout << sizeof(S);
quizQuestions.explanation: char a (1) + 3 padding + int b (4) + char c (1) + 3 padding = 12. Struct size rounds up to multiple of largest alignment (4).

quizQuestions.question: What does RAII stand for and what does it guarantee?
options: Resource Acquisition Is Initialization — resources freed in destructor
Runtime Allocation Is Immediate — fast memory allocation
Reference And Indirection Interface — pointer abstraction
Resource Allocation In Implementation — implementation detail
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: Resource Acquisition Is Initialization — resources freed in destructor
quizQuestions.explanation: RAII ties resource lifetime to object lifetime. Constructor acquires, destructor releases — even if exceptions occur.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: malloc calls the constructor of a C++ class.
quizQuestions.explanation: malloc only allocates raw memory. Only new calls constructors. Similarly, free doesn't call destructors.

quizQuestions.question: What happens if you use delete instead of delete[] for an array allocated with new[]?
quizQuestions.code:
int *arr = new int[10];
delete arr; // instead of delete[] arr;
options: Only the first element is deleted
quizQuestions.explanation: Mismatching new[] with delete is undefined behavior. For class types, it may only call one destructor instead of N.

chapters.title: Bit Manipulation
chapters.description: Bitwise operators, bit tricks, bit fields, and essential bit manipulation idioms used in systems and embedded programming.

topics.title: Bitwise Operators
topics.explanation:
C/C++ provides six bitwise operators:
- AND (&): Both bits must be 1
- OR (|): Either bit can be 1
- XOR (^): Bits must differ
- NOT (~): Flips all bits
- Left Shift (<<): Multiply by 2^n
- Right Shift (>>): Divide by 2^n (arithmetic for signed, logical for unsigned)
keyPoints: AND (&) is used for masking (extracting specific bits)
OR (|) is used for setting bits
XOR (^) is used for toggling bits and has unique properties (a^a=0, a^0=a)
Left shift by n multiplies by 2^n
Right shift of signed integers is implementation-defined (usually arithmetic)
Shifting by >= bit width is undefined behavior

codeExamples.title: Bitwise Operator Basics
codeExamples.explanation: Demonstrates all six bitwise operators with binary visualization using std::bitset.

commonMistakes: Confusing && (logical AND) with & (bitwise AND)
Shifting a 32-bit int by 32 or more (undefined behavior)
Right-shifting negative numbers (implementation-defined)
interviewTips: Know XOR properties cold: a^a=0, a^0=a, a^b^a=b
Understand operator precedence: & has lower precedence than ==
Use parentheses: if ((x & mask) == expected) — not x & mask == expected

topics.title: Essential Bit Tricks
topics.explanation: Common bit manipulation idioms that appear frequently in interviews and systems programming.
keyPoints: Set bit i:    n |= (1 << i)
Clear bit i:  n &= ~(1 << i)
Toggle bit i: n ^= (1 << i)
Check bit i:  (n >> i) & 1
Power of 2 check: n > 0 && (n & (n-1)) == 0
Lowest set bit: n & (-n)
Clear lowest set bit: n & (n-1)
Swap without temp: a ^= b; b ^= a; a ^= b;

codeExamples.title: Bit Manipulation Toolkit
codeExamples.explanation: Essential bit tricks: set/clear/toggle/check bits, power-of-2 check, Kernighan's bit counting, XOR swap.

commonMistakes: Forgetting parentheses: (1 << i) needs parens due to operator precedence
XOR swap fails when a and b are the same variable (zeroes it out)
Using signed int for bit manipulation (sign bit complications)
interviewTips: Memorize: n & (n-1) clears lowest set bit — used in many problems
Know Brian Kernighan's algorithm for counting set bits
Understand two's complement for negative number representation

topics.title: Bit Fields & Bitmasks
topics.explanation:
Bit fields allow packing multiple values into a single integer using specific bit ranges. Used extensively in network protocols, hardware registers, and flags.

Bitmasks define which bits to extract or modify. Common in permission systems (Unix file permissions), hardware programming, and feature flags.
keyPoints: Bit fields: struct { unsigned int flag : 1; unsigned int mode : 3; };
Bitmask pattern: flags |= MASK to set, flags &= ~MASK to clear
Use enum or constexpr for named bitmask constants
Bit fields are non-portable (ordering and padding are implementation-defined)
Prefer explicit bit manipulation over bit fields for portability

codeExamples.title: Bitmask Flags Pattern
codeExamples.explanation: Bitmask flags are used everywhere: file permissions, network protocol headers, GPU feature flags, CPU status registers.

commonMistakes: Relying on bit field ordering across compilers (it's implementation-defined)
Using signed types for bitmask operations
Not using constexpr/enum class for flag constants
interviewTips: Know real-world uses: TCP flags, UNIX permissions, hardware registers
Explain why enum class with overloaded operators is preferred for type-safe flags
Relevant for Qualcomm/Ericsson: protocol header parsing uses bitmasks extensively

topics.title: Endianness & Byte Ordering
topics.explanation:
Endianness defines how multi-byte values are stored in memory:
- Big-endian: Most significant byte at lowest address (network byte order)
- Little-endian: Least significant byte at lowest address (x86, ARM default)

Critical for network programming, file formats, and cross-platform serialization. Telecom protocols (5G, LTE) use big-endian (network byte order).
keyPoints: x86 and ARM (default) are little-endian
Network protocols use big-endian (network byte order)
Use htons/htonl to convert host to network byte order
Use ntohs/ntohl to convert network to host byte order
Endianness only matters for multi-byte types, not single bytes

codeExamples.title: Detecting and Handling Endianness
codeExamples.explanation: Endianness detection and byte swapping — essential for network/telecom programming.

commonMistakes: Sending multi-byte data over network without byte order conversion
Assuming all systems have the same endianness
Using memcpy between hosts with different endianness without conversion
interviewTips: Ericsson/Qualcomm/Nokia will ask about network byte order in telecom context
Know htons, htonl, ntohs, ntohl and when to use each
Be able to write byteSwap by hand

quizQuestions.question: What is the value of x?
quizQuestions.explanation: 1100 & 1010 = 1000 = 8. AND keeps only bits that are 1 in both operands.

quizQuestions.question: What does this expression evaluate to?
How do you clear bit i in integer n?
quizQuestions.explanation: ~(1 << i) creates a mask with all bits set except bit i. ANDing with n clears only bit i.

quizQuestions.question: How many times does this loop execute for n = 255?
quizQuestions.explanation: This is Kernighan's algorithm. 255 = 11111111 has 8 set bits. Each iteration clears one set bit, so the loop runs 8 times.

quizQuestions.question: In network programming (e.g., 5G protocol stack), what byte order is used?
quizQuestions.explanation: Network protocols use big-endian by convention (RFC 1700). Use htons/htonl to convert from host to network byte order.

chapters.title: Classes & OOP
chapters.description: Constructors, destructors, Rule of 3/5/0, virtual functions & vtable, inheritance, polymorphism, and the diamond problem.

topics.title: Constructors, Destructors & Rule of 3/5/0
topics.explanation:
Special member functions control object lifecycle:
- Default constructor: no args
- Parameterized constructor: custom init
- Copy constructor: T(const T&)
- Move constructor: T(T&&)
- Copy assignment: T& operator=(const T&)
- Move assignment: T& operator=(T&&)
- Destructor: ~T()

Rule of 3 (C++98): If you define destructor, copy constructor, or copy assignment — define all three.
Rule of 5 (C++11): Add move constructor and move assignment.
Rule of 0: Prefer using smart pointers/containers so you need NONE of these.
keyPoints: Compiler generates defaults unless you declare any special member function
If you declare a destructor, compiler won't generate move operations
= default forces compiler generation; = delete prevents usage
Use member initializer lists for efficiency (avoids default-then-assign)
Delegating constructors (C++11) can call other constructors

codeExamples.title: Rule of 5 Implementation
codeExamples.explanation: Complete Rule of 5 with copy-and-swap idiom. Move constructor steals resources instead of copying — O(1) vs O(n).

commonMistakes: Shallow copy when class owns resources (double-free!)
Not marking move constructor noexcept (prevents use with std::vector)
Forgetting to nullify moved-from object's pointers
interviewTips: Know copy-and-swap idiom and why it provides strong exception safety
Explain why Rule of 0 is preferred in modern C++
Know that std::move doesn't move — it casts to rvalue reference

topics.title: Virtual Functions & vtable
topics.explanation:
Virtual functions enable runtime polymorphism. When a function is declared virtual, calls through base pointers/references are dispatched to the correct derived class version at runtime.

Implementation: compiler creates a vtable (array of function pointers) for each class with virtual functions. Each object gets a hidden vptr pointing to its class's vtable.
keyPoints: virtual keyword enables dynamic dispatch
override keyword (C++11) catches errors if base function doesn't exist
vtable: per-class table of function pointers
vptr: per-object hidden pointer to the vtable (adds sizeof(void*) to object)
Virtual destructor is essential if deleting through base pointer
Pure virtual (= 0) makes a class abstract
Virtual function call has slight overhead: indirect function call through vptr

codeExamples.title: Virtual Functions and vtable Mechanism
codeExamples.explanation: Virtual functions enable polymorphism. Without virtual, the base class version would always be called through a base pointer.

commonMistakes: Forgetting virtual destructor in base class (undefined behavior when deleting through base pointer)
Calling virtual functions in constructor/destructor (no polymorphism!)
Not using override keyword, silently creating a new function instead of overriding
interviewTips: Draw the vtable layout for a class hierarchy
Explain the cost of virtual functions (indirect call, cache miss potential)
Know that virtual functions in constructors resolve to the current class, not derived

topics.title: Inheritance & Diamond Problem
topics.explanation:
C++ supports multiple inheritance, which can lead to the diamond problem: if B and C both inherit from A, and D inherits from both B and C, D gets two copies of A's members.

Solution: virtual inheritance ensures only one copy of the base class exists.
keyPoints: public inheritance: IS-A relationship
protected inheritance: changes public members to protected in derived
private inheritance: IS-IMPLEMENTED-IN-TERMS-OF relationship
Diamond problem: D inherits A through both B and C
Virtual inheritance: class B : virtual public A — single copy of A
Virtual base classes add overhead (virtual base table pointer)

codeExamples.title: Diamond Problem & Virtual Inheritance
codeExamples.explanation: Virtual inheritance ensures one copy of Animal in Bat. Without virtual, bat.age would be ambiguous.

commonMistakes: Forgetting virtual keyword leads to ambiguous member access
Not understanding that virtual base class constructors are called by the most derived class
Overusing multiple inheritance — prefer composition
interviewTips: Be able to explain the diamond problem with a diagram
Know that virtual inheritance adds a vbptr (virtual base table pointer)
Discuss composition over inheritance as a design principle

topics.title: Copy & Move Semantics
topics.explanation:
Copy semantics create independent copies of objects. Move semantics (C++11) transfer resources from one object to another without copying, converting expensive deep copies into cheap pointer swaps.

Key: std::move doesn't actually move anything — it's just a cast to rvalue reference (T&&), which enables the move constructor/assignment to be called.
keyPoints: Lvalue: named object, has address. Rvalue: temporary, about to be destroyed
std::move() casts to rvalue reference — enables move semantics
Move constructor/assignment should leave source in valid but unspecified state
Mark move operations noexcept for optimal container performance
RVO (Return Value Optimization) often eliminates copies/moves entirely
std::forward preserves value category for perfect forwarding

codeExamples.title: Move Semantics Performance
codeExamples.explanation: Move semantics turn O(n) copies into O(1) resource transfers. The source object is left in a valid but empty state.

commonMistakes: Using an object after std::move (it's in unspecified state)
Thinking std::move actually moves something (it's just a cast)
Not marking move operations noexcept (vector will copy instead of move!)
interviewTips: Explain lvalue vs rvalue and how && (rvalue reference) works
Know that RVO/NRVO can eliminate copies entirely — even better than move
Explain perfect forwarding with std::forward and why it matters for templates

topics.title: Operator Overloading & static Members
topics.explanation:
C++ allows most operators to be overloaded for user-defined types, enabling natural syntax. Some operators must be member functions (=, [], (), ->), others are better as free functions (+, -, ==, <<).

Static members belong to the class, not instances. Static member functions can only access static members.
keyPoints: Overload operators to make types behave like built-in types
operator= must be a member function
operator<< (stream output) should be a friend or free function
Static data members: shared across all instances, defined outside class
Static member functions: no 'this' pointer, can't access non-static members
Spaceship operator <=> (C++20) generates all comparison operators

codeExamples.title: Operator Overloading Example
codeExamples.explanation: Operator overloading enables natural syntax. Static members are shared across all instances.

commonMistakes: Overloading operator&& or operator|| (breaks short-circuit evaluation)
Forgetting to return *this from assignment operator
Not defining static members outside the class
interviewTips: Know which operators can/cannot be overloaded
Explain why operator<< is usually a friend function (left operand is ostream)
Know the C++20 spaceship operator <=> for automatic comparison generation

quizQuestions.question: What is the Rule of 5 in C++11?
options: If you define any of: destructor, copy constructor, copy assignment, move constructor, move assignment — define all five
A class should have at most 5 member variables
Inheritance hierarchy should be at most 5 levels deep
Functions should have at most 5 parameters
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: If you define any of: destructor, copy constructor, copy assignment, move constructor, move assignment — define all five
quizQuestions.explanation: The Rule of 5 extends the Rule of 3 to include move semantics. If you manage resources, you likely need all five.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: Virtual functions can exhibit polymorphic behavior when called inside a constructor.
quizQuestions.explanation: In a constructor, the vtable points to the current class, not the derived class. Virtual calls resolve to the constructor's own class version.

quizQuestions.question: What happens if a base class has a non-virtual destructor and you delete a derived object through a base pointer?
quizQuestions.explanation: Deleting a derived object through a base pointer without a virtual destructor is undefined behavior per the standard.

quizQuestions.question: What is the output?
quizQuestions.explanation: f() is virtual. p points to a B object, so B::f() is called through dynamic dispatch via the vtable.

quizQuestions.question: What does std::move(x) actually do?
options: Moves the data from x to a new location
Casts x to an rvalue reference
Copies x and then deletes the original
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: Casts x to an rvalue reference
quizQuestions.explanation: std::move is just a cast to T&&. It enables the move constructor/assignment to be selected, but doesn't move anything itself.

chapters.title: Templates & Generic Programming
chapters.description: Function/class templates, specialization, SFINAE, variadic templates, CRTP, and C++20 concepts.

topics.title: Function & Class Templates
topics.explanation: Templates enable writing generic code that works with any type. The compiler generates specific versions (instantiations) for each type used. Function templates deduce types from arguments; class templates require explicit specification (before C++17 CTAD).
keyPoints: template<typename T> syntax
Implicit type deduction for functions
Class Template Argument Deduction (CTAD) in C++17
Templates are compiled at instantiation, not definition
Code bloat risk: each instantiation generates new code

codeExamples.title: Generic Max Function
codeExamples.explanation: Templates generate type-specific code at compile time. Zero runtime overhead.

commonMistakes: Defining templates in .cpp files (linker error — must be in headers)
Not understanding that each instantiation is separate code
interviewTips: Explain how templates differ from Java/C# generics (compile-time vs runtime)
Know about template argument deduction rules

topics.title: Template Specialization
topics.explanation: Full specialization provides a completely different implementation for a specific type. Partial specialization (class templates only) specializes based on patterns.
keyPoints: Full specialization: template<> class Foo<int> {...}
Partial specialization works on class templates, not function templates
Use overloading instead of function template specialization
Specialization must be declared after the primary template

codeExamples.title: Template Specialization
codeExamples.explanation: Specialization customizes template behavior for specific types. Essential for optimizing string comparisons, pointer handling, etc.

commonMistakes: Trying to partially specialize function templates
Forgetting template<> for full specialization
interviewTips: Know the difference between specialization and overloading
Explain when to use specialization vs if constexpr (C++17)

topics.title: Variadic Templates & Fold Expressions
topics.explanation: Variadic templates accept any number of template arguments using parameter packs (typename... Args). Combined with fold expressions (C++17), they enable powerful compile-time operations on argument lists.
keyPoints: Parameter pack: typename... Args, Args... args
sizeof...(Args) gives the number of arguments
Fold expressions (C++17): (args + ...) folds over all args
Used in std::tuple, std::make_unique, std::variant
Recursive unpacking pattern for pre-C++17 code

codeExamples.title: Variadic Template Print
codeExamples.explanation: Variadic templates with fold expressions provide concise, type-safe variadic functions — the modern replacement for C's va_args.

commonMistakes: Forgetting the base case in recursive unpacking (pre-C++17)
Not understanding pack expansion rules
interviewTips: Know how std::make_unique uses perfect forwarding with variadic templates
Explain the difference between variadic templates and C varargs (type safety)

topics.title: CRTP & Concepts (C++20)
topics.explanation:
CRTP (Curiously Recurring Template Pattern): A class inherits from a template instantiated with itself. Enables static polymorphism (no vtable overhead).

Concepts (C++20): Named constraints on template parameters. Replace SFINAE with readable syntax.
keyPoints: CRTP: class Derived : public Base<Derived>
Static polymorphism: dispatch resolved at compile time, no virtual overhead
Concepts: template<Sortable T> replaces template<typename T> + SFINAE
requires clause for ad-hoc constraints
Concepts produce better error messages than SFINAE

codeExamples.title: CRTP and Concepts
codeExamples.explanation: CRTP provides polymorphism without virtual function overhead. Concepts constrain templates with readable, meaningful error messages.

commonMistakes: Infinite recursion in CRTP if drawImpl is missing
Overcomplicating SFINAE when concepts would suffice
interviewTips: Know CRTP use cases: static polymorphism, mixin classes
Nvidia/Google increasingly ask about C++20 concepts

quizQuestions.question: Where should template definitions be placed?
options: In a separate .tpp file linked at runtime
quizQuestions.explanation: Templates must be visible at the point of instantiation. Placing them in .cpp files causes linker errors because the compiler can't instantiate them from other translation units.

quizQuestions.question: What is CRTP used for?
quizQuestions.explanation: CRTP achieves polymorphism at compile time through template instantiation, avoiding the overhead of virtual function dispatch.

quizQuestions.question: What does sizeof...(args) return for: template<typename... Args> void f(Args... args) called as f(1, 2.0, "hi")?
quizQuestions.explanation: sizeof...(args) returns the number of arguments in the parameter pack, which is 3.

quizQuestions.question: What problem do C++20 concepts solve?
options: Unreadable SFINAE error messages and constraints
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: Unreadable SFINAE error messages and constraints
quizQuestions.explanation: Concepts provide named, readable constraints for template parameters, replacing cryptic SFINAE techniques and producing clear error messages.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: Function templates can be partially specialized.
quizQuestions.explanation: Only class templates support partial specialization. For function templates, use overloading instead.

chapters.title: STL Containers & Iterators
chapters.description: Vector, map, unordered_map, set, deque, iterator categories, and iterator invalidation rules.

topics.title: Sequence Containers: vector, deque, list
topics.explanation:
vector: Dynamic array, contiguous memory, O(1) random access, amortized O(1) push_back. The most used container.
deque: Double-ended queue, O(1) push_front and push_back, not fully contiguous.
list: Doubly linked list, O(1) insert/erase anywhere with iterator, O(n) access.
keyPoints: vector: contiguous memory = cache-friendly, capacity doubles on growth
deque: fixed-size blocks, O(1) front/back operations
list: no random access, but O(1) splice and iterator stability
Prefer vector by default — it's fastest for most use cases
reserve() prevents reallocations if you know the size upfront

codeExamples.title: Container Comparison
codeExamples.explanation: Choose vector for most cases, deque for double-ended operations, list only when you need stable iterators and constant-time splicing.

commonMistakes: Using list when vector would be faster (cache locality matters more than O(1) insert)
Not calling reserve() on vector when size is known
interviewTips: Know the time complexity of every operation for each container
Explain why vector is usually faster than list even for middle insertions (cache effects)

topics.title: Associative Containers: map, set, unordered_map
topics.explanation:
map/set: Red-black tree based, O(log n) operations, keys are sorted.
unordered_map/unordered_set: Hash table based, O(1) average operations, no ordering.
multimap/multiset: Allow duplicate keys.
keyPoints: map: sorted keys, O(log n) lookup/insert/erase
unordered_map: hash table, O(1) average, O(n) worst case
map preserves insertion order by key; unordered_map does not
Custom comparators for map, custom hash for unordered_map
emplace() constructs in-place, avoiding unnecessary copies

codeExamples.title: map vs unordered_map
codeExamples.explanation: Use map when you need sorted keys; unordered_map for fastest average lookup. Both are critical for interviews.

commonMistakes: Using [] on a const map (it inserts default if key missing!)
Not providing hash function for custom types in unordered_map
interviewTips: Know when to use map vs unordered_map (sorted needs vs speed)
Be ready to implement a simple hash table from scratch

topics.title: Iterator Categories & Invalidation
topics.explanation:
Iterator categories (weakest to strongest): Input, Output, Forward, Bidirectional, Random Access, Contiguous (C++20).

Iterator invalidation: operations like push_back, erase, insert can invalidate existing iterators. Rules differ per container.
keyPoints: vector: any reallocation invalidates ALL iterators; insert/erase invalidates from that point onward
deque: insert/erase at ends keeps iterators valid (mostly)
list/set/map: only iterators to erased elements are invalidated
unordered_map: rehashing invalidates all iterators
Use returned iterator from erase() for safe removal while iterating

codeExamples.title: Safe Erase While Iterating
codeExamples.explanation: Iterator invalidation is a major source of bugs. Always use the return value of erase() or the erase-remove idiom.

commonMistakes: Erasing from a vector while iterating with range-based for
Assuming iterators are valid after push_back (they're not if reallocation occurs)
interviewTips: Memorize iterator invalidation rules for vector, map, unordered_map
Know the erase-remove idiom — it's a classic interview question

quizQuestions.question: Which STL container provides O(1) random access and contiguous memory?
quizQuestions.explanation: vector uses contiguous memory like an array, providing O(1) random access via operator[].

quizQuestions.question: What happens when you use map::operator[] with a key that doesn't exist?
quizQuestions.explanation: operator[] inserts a default-constructed value if the key doesn't exist. Use find() or at() to avoid unintended insertion.

quizQuestions.question: After calling push_back on a vector, which iterators are guaranteed to remain valid?
quizQuestions.explanation: If push_back triggers reallocation, ALL iterators, pointers, and references are invalidated. If no reallocation, only end() is invalidated.

quizQuestions.question: What is the time complexity of std::map::find()?
quizQuestions.explanation: std::map is implemented as a red-black tree (balanced BST), so find() is O(log n). Use unordered_map for O(1) average.

chapters.title: STL Algorithms & Lambdas
chapters.description: Key STL algorithms, lambda expressions, std::function, and functional programming patterns.

topics.title: Essential Algorithms: sort, find, transform, accumulate
topics.explanation: STL provides 100+ algorithms in <algorithm> and <numeric>. They operate on iterators, making them container-agnostic.
keyPoints: sort: O(n log n) introsort, works on random-access iterators
find/find_if: linear search, returns iterator to first match
transform: applies function to each element, stores result
accumulate: reduces range to single value (fold)
All algorithms work on any container via iterators

codeExamples.title: Common STL Algorithms
codeExamples.explanation: STL algorithms + lambdas = expressive, efficient code. Know these cold for interviews.

commonMistakes: Sorting a list with std::sort (list has its own sort method)
Forgetting initial value in accumulate (can cause type mismatch)
interviewTips: Know the complexity of major algorithms
Prefer STL algorithms over hand-written loops

topics.title: Lambda Expressions
topics.explanation:
Lambdas (C++11) are anonymous function objects. Syntax: [capture](params) -> return_type { body }

Capture modes: [=] by value, [&] by reference, [x, &y] specific captures, [this] captures this pointer.
keyPoints: [=] captures all by value, [&] all by reference
[x] captures x by value, [&x] by reference
Mutable lambdas: [x]() mutable { x++; } allows modifying captured values
Generic lambdas (C++14): [](auto x) { ... }
Lambdas without captures can convert to function pointers

codeExamples.title: Lambda Capture Modes
codeExamples.explanation: Lambdas are essential modern C++. Capture modes control which outer variables are accessible and how.

commonMistakes: Capturing by reference when the lambda outlives the variable (dangling reference)
Forgetting mutable when modifying captured-by-value variables
interviewTips: Know all capture modes and when to use each
Explain how lambdas are implemented (compiler generates a functor class)

quizQuestions.question: What does [&] in a lambda capture clause mean?
quizQuestions.explanation: [&] captures all local variables used in the lambda body by reference.

quizQuestions.question: What is the output?
quizQuestions.explanation: greater<int>() sorts in descending order: {5,4,3,1,1}. v[0]=5, v[4]=1.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: A lambda with no captures can be implicitly converted to a function pointer.
quizQuestions.explanation: Non-capturing lambdas are convertible to function pointers of matching signature, since they don't need a closure.

quizQuestions.question: What is the time complexity of std::sort?
options: O(n log n) average, O(n^2) worst
quizQuestions.explanation: std::sort uses introsort: quicksort + heapsort fallback + insertion sort for small ranges. Guaranteed O(n log n).

chapters.title: Concurrency & Multithreading
chapters.description: Threads, mutexes, atomics, condition variables, async/futures, and common concurrency patterns.

topics.title: std::thread, mutex, lock_guard
topics.explanation: std::thread launches a new thread of execution. Shared data must be protected with mutexes. lock_guard/unique_lock provide RAII-based locking.
keyPoints: std::thread t(func, args...) launches a thread
Must call t.join() or t.detach() before thread object is destroyed
std::mutex protects shared data (lock/unlock)
std::lock_guard: RAII lock — locks in constructor, unlocks in destructor
std::unique_lock: flexible RAII lock — supports deferred locking, condition variables
std::scoped_lock (C++17): locks multiple mutexes without deadlock

codeExamples.title: Thread-Safe Counter
codeExamples.explanation: lock_guard ensures the mutex is always unlocked, even if an exception occurs. Without it, forgetting to unlock causes deadlock.

commonMistakes: Forgetting to join or detach threads (program terminates!)
Locking multiple mutexes in different order (deadlock)
Data races: accessing shared data without protection
interviewTips: Know the difference between lock_guard, unique_lock, and scoped_lock
Explain deadlock conditions (mutual exclusion, hold-and-wait, no preemption, circular wait)
Qualcomm/Ericsson heavily test concurrency for telecom systems

topics.title: Atomic Operations & Condition Variables
topics.explanation: std::atomic provides lock-free thread-safe operations on individual variables. std::condition_variable enables threads to wait for events (producer-consumer pattern).
keyPoints: atomic<int>: lock-free increment, compare-exchange, load, store
Faster than mutex for simple operations on single variables
condition_variable: wait/notify pattern for thread coordination
Always use condition_variable with a predicate to handle spurious wakeups
memory_order controls instruction reordering (seq_cst is default and safest)

codeExamples.title: Producer-Consumer Pattern
codeExamples.explanation: Producer-consumer is a classic concurrency pattern. condition_variable with predicate handles spurious wakeups correctly.

commonMistakes: Not using a predicate with cv.wait() (spurious wakeups!)
Using atomic for complex operations that need multiple steps (use mutex instead)
interviewTips: Know when to use atomic vs mutex (single variable vs compound operations)
Explain memory ordering: seq_cst vs relaxed vs acquire/release

quizQuestions.question: What happens if you don't call join() or detach() on a std::thread before it's destroyed?
quizQuestions.explanation: If a joinable thread's destructor runs, it calls std::terminate(). Always join or detach.

quizQuestions.question: Why should you always use a predicate with condition_variable::wait()?
options: To prevent spurious wakeups from causing bugs
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: To prevent spurious wakeups from causing bugs
quizQuestions.explanation: Condition variables can wake up spuriously (without notify). The predicate re-checks the actual condition.

quizQuestions.question: Which C++17 feature locks multiple mutexes without risking deadlock?
quizQuestions.explanation: std::scoped_lock uses a deadlock avoidance algorithm to lock multiple mutexes safely.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: std::atomic operations require a mutex for thread safety.
quizQuestions.explanation: atomic operations are lock-free and thread-safe by themselves. That's their purpose — avoiding mutexes for simple operations.

chapters.title: Modern C++ Features (C++11/14/17/20)
chapters.description: auto, structured bindings, optional/variant, string_view, constexpr, and other essential modern features.

topics.title: auto, decltype, and Structured Bindings
topics.explanation: auto deduces types from initializers. decltype gives the declared type of an expression. Structured bindings (C++17) decompose structs/pairs/tuples into named variables.
keyPoints: auto x = 5; // int. auto& r = x; // int&
decltype(expr) gives the type of expr without evaluating it
auto in return types (C++14): auto func() { return 42; }
Structured bindings: auto [x, y] = make_pair(1, 2);
auto with const/references: const auto& is common for range-based for

codeExamples.title: Modern Type Deduction
codeExamples.explanation: auto and structured bindings make code cleaner without sacrificing type safety — types are still statically determined.

commonMistakes: auto x = {1}; deduces initializer_list in C++11 but int in C++17 for auto x{1}
Forgetting & with auto in range-for (copies instead of references)
interviewTips: Know when auto is appropriate vs explicit types
Explain that auto is a compile-time feature — no runtime cost

topics.title: std::optional, std::variant, std::string_view
topics.explanation:
optional<T>: May or may not hold a value (replaces nullable pointers/sentinel values).
variant<Types...>: Type-safe union (replaces C unions).
string_view: Non-owning view of a string (zero-copy).
keyPoints: optional: has_value(), value(), value_or(default)
variant: holds one of several types, visited with std::visit
string_view: lightweight, non-owning reference to string data
string_view avoids copies when passing substrings or string literals
Never store string_view that outlives the underlying string

codeExamples.title: Optional, Variant, StringView
codeExamples.explanation: These types replace error-prone C patterns: optional vs NULL pointers, variant vs unions, string_view vs const char*.

commonMistakes: Storing string_view that outlives the source string (dangling)
Using get<T> on variant without checking which type it holds (throws bad_variant_access)
interviewTips: Know the difference between optional and nullable pointer
Explain when string_view is safe vs dangerous

quizQuestions.question: What does auto [a, b] = make_pair(1, 2); do?
options: Decomposes a pair into variables a and b
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: Decomposes a pair into variables a and b
quizQuestions.explanation: Structured bindings (C++17) decompose aggregate types into named variables.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: std::string_view owns the string data it refers to.
quizQuestions.explanation: string_view is a non-owning reference. The underlying string must outlive the string_view.

quizQuestions.question: What does std::optional<int> return when the value is absent?
quizQuestions.explanation: nullopt represents the absence of a value in an optional. has_value() returns false.

quizQuestions.question: What is the advantage of std::variant over a C union?
options: Type safety — tracks which type is active
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: Type safety — tracks which type is active
quizQuestions.explanation: variant knows which type it currently holds and throws bad_variant_access if you access the wrong type. C unions have no such safety.

chapters.title: Type Casting & Type System
chapters.description: static_cast, dynamic_cast, const_cast, reinterpret_cast, and strict aliasing.

topics.title: C++ Cast Operators
topics.explanation:
C++ provides four explicit casts, each with specific semantics:
- static_cast: compile-time checked conversions (numeric, up/downcasts)
- dynamic_cast: runtime-checked polymorphic downcasts
- const_cast: adds/removes const qualifier
- reinterpret_cast: bit-pattern reinterpretation (dangerous)
keyPoints: static_cast: most common, compile-time type checking
dynamic_cast: requires virtual functions, returns nullptr on failure (pointers) or throws bad_cast (references)
const_cast: only cast that can remove const; UB if object was originally const
reinterpret_cast: type-punning, pointer to integer conversions
Prefer C++ casts over C-style (T)x — they're searchable and explicit

codeExamples.title: Four C++ Casts
codeExamples.explanation: Each cast serves a specific purpose. Use the weakest cast that works to maximize safety.

commonMistakes: Using reinterpret_cast when static_cast would work
Using dynamic_cast on non-polymorphic types (no virtual functions)
Modifying through const_cast when original was actually const (UB)
interviewTips: Explain when each cast should be used
Know that dynamic_cast has runtime overhead (RTTI)
Understand strict aliasing rule and how reinterpret_cast can violate it

quizQuestions.question: Which cast should you use for a safe downcast in an inheritance hierarchy?
quizQuestions.explanation: dynamic_cast performs a runtime check and returns nullptr if the cast fails, making it safe for downcasting.

quizQuestions.question: What does dynamic_cast return when a pointer cast fails?
quizQuestions.explanation: For pointer casts, dynamic_cast returns nullptr on failure. For reference casts, it throws std::bad_cast.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: C-style casts are preferred over C++ named casts in modern code.
quizQuestions.explanation: C++ named casts are preferred: they're explicit, searchable (grep), and each has specific semantics. C-style casts can silently perform dangerous conversions.

chapters.title: Error Handling & Exceptions
chapters.description: Exception hierarchy, noexcept, exception safety guarantees, and RAII for exception safety.

topics.title: Exceptions and noexcept
topics.explanation: C++ exceptions unwind the stack, calling destructors for all local objects (RAII). noexcept specifier indicates a function won't throw — enables optimizations and is required for move operations used with containers.
keyPoints: throw expression creates and throws an exception object
catch blocks handle exceptions by type, catch(...) catches all
Stack unwinding calls destructors for all local objects
noexcept functions call std::terminate if they throw
Move constructors MUST be noexcept for optimal vector performance
Exception safety levels: nothrow, strong, basic

codeExamples.title: Exception Safety with RAII
codeExamples.explanation: RAII + exceptions = automatic cleanup. Resource r is always released, even when an exception is thrown.

commonMistakes: Throwing in destructors (terminates the program during stack unwinding)
Not catching by const reference (slicing or unnecessary copies)
Forgetting noexcept on move operations
interviewTips: Know the three exception safety guarantees: basic (no leaks), strong (rollback), nothrow
Explain why destructors must be noexcept
Discuss error codes vs exceptions tradeoffs (Ericsson/Qualcomm embedded contexts may prefer error codes)

quizQuestions.question: What happens if a noexcept function throws an exception?
quizQuestions.explanation: If a noexcept function throws, std::terminate() is called immediately. The exception does not propagate.

quizQuestions.question: Which exception safety guarantee means the operation either succeeds completely or has no effect?
quizQuestions.explanation: Strong guarantee = commit or rollback. The operation either completes fully or leaves the state unchanged. copy-and-swap idiom provides this.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: Exceptions should be caught by const reference.
quizQuestions.explanation: Catching by const reference avoids slicing (if catching a base class) and unnecessary copies.

chapters.title: Preprocessor & Compilation
chapters.description: Macros, include guards, compilation stages, linking, ODR, and translation units.

topics.title: Macros, Include Guards, and Compilation Pipeline
topics.explanation:
The C/C++ build process: Preprocessing (#include, #define expansion) → Compilation (source to object) → Assembly → Linking (object files + libraries → executable).

Macros are text substitution. Include guards (#ifndef/#define/#endif) or #pragma once prevent multiple inclusion.
keyPoints: Preprocessor: text substitution before compilation
Include guards prevent multiple definitions from multiple #include
#pragma once is non-standard but universally supported and simpler
Macros don't respect scope or types — prefer constexpr/inline/templates
ODR: One Definition Rule — each entity defined exactly once across all TUs
inline functions/variables (C++17) are exempt from ODR across TUs

codeExamples.title: Build Pipeline & Macros
codeExamples.explanation: Modern C++ replaces most macro uses with constexpr, inline, and templates. Use macros only for conditional compilation and debug logging.

commonMistakes: Macro arguments not parenthesized: #define MUL(a,b) a*b → MUL(1+2,3) = 1+2*3 = 7
Forgetting include guards (multiple definition errors)
Defining non-inline functions in headers (ODR violation)
interviewTips: Explain the full compilation pipeline for a C++ program
Know the difference between compile-time and link-time errors
Understand why templates must be in headers

quizQuestions.question: What is the purpose of #pragma once?
options: Prevent multiple inclusion of a header
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: Prevent multiple inclusion of a header
quizQuestions.explanation: #pragma once ensures a header file is included at most once per translation unit, preventing redefinition errors.

quizQuestions.question: What macro pitfall exists in: #define SQUARE(x) x * x
options: Result is 5 due to operator precedence
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: Result is 5 due to operator precedence
quizQuestions.explanation: SQUARE(1+2) expands to 1+2 * 1+2 = 1+2+2 = 5. Fix: #define SQUARE(x) ((x) * (x))

quizQuestions.question: In which compilation stage does #include expansion happen?
quizQuestions.explanation: The preprocessor handles all # directives (include, define, ifdef) before the compiler sees the code.

chapters.title: Storage Classes & Linkage
chapters.description: static, extern, mutable, thread_local, storage duration, and linkage types.

topics.title: Storage Duration & Linkage
topics.explanation:
Storage duration: automatic (stack), static (global/static), dynamic (heap), thread (thread_local).
Linkage: no linkage (local), internal (static/anonymous namespace), external (extern).
keyPoints: static local: initialized once, persists across calls
static global/function: internal linkage (file-scoped)
extern: external linkage, declaration without definition
thread_local: each thread gets its own copy
mutable: allows modification in const member functions
Anonymous namespace is the C++ way to get internal linkage (preferred over static)

codeExamples.title: Storage Classes Demo
codeExamples.explanation: Each storage class and qualifier serves a specific purpose. static local is the most commonly asked about in interviews.

commonMistakes: Confusing static at file scope (internal linkage) with static in a class (shared member)
Forgetting that static locals are initialized only once (not thread-safe before C++11)
interviewTips: Explain the Meyers Singleton: static local with lazy initialization
Know that C++11 guarantees thread-safe initialization of static locals

quizQuestions.question: How many times is a static local variable initialized?
quizQuestions.explanation: Static local variables are initialized the first time execution reaches their declaration. In C++11+, this is thread-safe.

quizQuestions.question: What does the mutable keyword allow?
options: Member can be modified in const methods
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: Member can be modified in const methods
quizQuestions.explanation: mutable lets a data member be modified even in const member functions. Used for caches, mutexes, debug counters.

quizQuestions.question: What does thread_local guarantee?
options: Each thread has its own copy of the variable
Variable is allocated on the stack
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: Each thread has its own copy of the variable
quizQuestions.explanation: thread_local creates a separate instance of the variable for each thread, avoiding shared state.

chapters.title: Undefined Behavior & Pitfalls
chapters.description: Common sources of undefined behavior and dangerous patterns every C/C++ developer must know.

topics.title: Common Sources of Undefined Behavior
topics.explanation:
Undefined behavior (UB) means the C/C++ standard places NO requirements on the program's behavior. Compilers may optimize assuming UB never occurs, leading to surprising results.

Most security vulnerabilities in C/C++ stem from UB: buffer overflows, use-after-free, null dereference.
keyPoints: Signed integer overflow is UB (unsigned wraps around)
Dereferencing null or dangling pointers
Buffer overflow: accessing beyond array bounds
Use-after-free and double-free
Shifting by >= bit width of the type
Reading uninitialized variables
Modifying a const object through const_cast
Data races: unsynchronized concurrent access to shared data
Violating strict aliasing rule

codeExamples.title: Common UB Examples
codeExamples.explanation: UB makes programs unpredictable. Compilers exploit UB for optimization — a signed overflow check may be removed entirely.

commonMistakes: Assuming signed integer overflow wraps like unsigned
Assuming UB will crash — it might 'work' today and fail tomorrow
Not using sanitizers (ASan, UBSan) during development
interviewTips: Know at least 5-6 sources of UB by heart
Explain why compilers exploit UB for optimization
Mention sanitizers: -fsanitize=address,undefined

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following is undefined behavior?
quizQuestions.explanation: Signed integer overflow is UB. Unsigned overflow wraps around (well-defined). Float truncation is well-defined. Division by non-zero is fine.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: If a program has undefined behavior, the compiler is allowed to do ANYTHING, including formatting your hard drive.
quizQuestions.explanation: Technically correct per the standard. In practice, compilers usually just optimize aggressively, but UB means NO guarantees whatsoever.

quizQuestions.question: What tool helps detect undefined behavior at runtime?
quizQuestions.explanation: UBSan is a compiler sanitizer that detects undefined behavior at runtime with minimal overhead. Use with -fsanitize=undefined.

chapters.title: Systems Programming Essentials
chapters.description: volatile, IPC, signal handling, sockets, and cache — essential for telecom/embedded roles at Ericsson/Qualcomm.

topics.title: volatile, IPC & Sockets
topics.explanation:
volatile tells the compiler the value may change outside the program's control (hardware registers, signal handlers, memory-mapped I/O). It prevents optimization of reads/writes.

IPC mechanisms: pipes, shared memory, message queues, sockets, signals. Sockets are the foundation of network programming in telecom.
keyPoints: volatile prevents compiler from caching variable in register
volatile does NOT provide atomicity or thread safety (use atomic for that)
Sockets: AF_INET + SOCK_STREAM (TCP) or SOCK_DGRAM (UDP)
Shared memory: fastest IPC, but requires synchronization
Signals: asynchronous notifications (SIGINT, SIGTERM, SIGUSR1)
In telecom: socket programming is essential for protocol stacks

codeExamples.title: Volatile and Socket Basics
codeExamples.explanation: volatile is for hardware/signal handlers, NOT threading. Sockets are the foundation of telecom network stacks.

commonMistakes: Using volatile for thread safety (it's not atomic!)
Not converting byte order with htons/htonl for network communication
Forgetting to close sockets (resource leak)
interviewTips: Ericsson/Nokia/Qualcomm will ask about socket programming and protocol stacks
Know select/poll/epoll for handling multiple connections
Explain the difference between TCP and UDP for telecom applications
Know volatile vs atomic: volatile prevents optimization, atomic provides thread safety

quizQuestions.question: True or False: volatile provides thread safety for shared variables.
quizQuestions.explanation: volatile only prevents compiler optimization. It does NOT provide atomicity or memory ordering. Use std::atomic for thread safety.

quizQuestions.question: Which function converts host byte order to network byte order for a 16-bit value?
quizQuestions.explanation: htons = Host TO Network Short (16-bit). htonl is for 32-bit. ntohs/ntohl are the reverse conversions.

quizQuestions.question: Which IPC mechanism is fastest for inter-process communication on the same machine?
quizQuestions.explanation: Shared memory avoids kernel-mediated copies. Data is directly accessible by both processes. Requires explicit synchronization (semaphores/mutexes).
